coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of iron and carbon-quantum-dot co-decorated graphitic carbon nitride photocatalysts for carbamazepine degradation

coredata.dc:description: In this study, a one-step hydrothermal preparation strategy was employed to synthesize a graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) composite photocatalyst co-decorated with Fe sites and carbon quantum dots (Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4). Through a series of characterisation techniques, the successful integration of Fe atoms and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto g-C3N4 was achieved. Moreover, the presence of Fe, specifically Fe–N bonds, enhanced the charge-carrier transfer. With carbamazepine (CBZ) as the model pollutant, Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 increased the photocatalytic activity with efficient peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation under solar light irradiation, achieving 100 % CBZ degradation in 15 min over a wide pH range (3–9). Doping of the catalyst with Fe and CQDs resulted in a narrowing of the catalyst bandgap (2.25 eV) compared to that of g-C3N4 (2.79 eV), confirming the promotion of photo-induced charge separation. The CBZ degradation pathways were elucidated by ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography and high-definition mass spectrometry. The Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 composite was found to possess substantial potential for the efficient degradation of pollutants.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of iron and carbon-quantum-dot co-decorated graphitic carbon nitride photocatalysts for carbamazepine degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a one-step hydrothermal preparation strategy was employed to synthesize a graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) composite photocatalyst co-decorated with Fe sites and carbon quantum dots (Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4). Through a series of characterisation techniques, the successful integration of Fe atoms and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto g-C3N4 was achieved. Moreover, the presence of Fe, specifically Fe–N bonds, enhanced the charge-carrier transfer. With carbamazepine (CBZ) as the model pollutant, Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 increased the photocatalytic activity with efficient peroxymonosulfate (PMS) activation under solar light irradiation, achieving 100 % CBZ degradation in 15 min over a wide pH range (3–9). Doping of the catalyst with Fe and CQDs resulted in a narrowing of the catalyst bandgap (2.25 eV) compared to that of g-C3N4 (2.79 eV), confirming the promotion of photo-induced charge separation. The CBZ degradation pathways were elucidated by ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography and high-definition mass spectrometry. The Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 composite was found to possess substantial potential for the efficient degradation of pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: A significant amount of toxic and recalcitrant pollutants is generated and accumulated in aquatic environments owing to the surge in global population, rapid industrial advancement, and global warming [1,2]. Given the persistence of pharmaceuticals and personal care products in the environment, massive emissions of these products have perpetually contaminated aquatic ecosystems [3]. Carbamazepine (CBZ), an antipsychotic drug commonly used to treat epilepsy [4], has poor biodegradability and is frequently identified as a pharmacological pollutant in various water bodies, posing a significant threat to aquatic environments [5]. Conventional biochemical treatment processes are ineffective in removing CBZ, owing to its symmetrical N-containing aromatic heterocyclic structure [6]. Therefore, the development of a facile and effective method for CBZ degradation is a significant challenge.

Photocatalysis is a potent method for harnessing solar energy to resolve energy crises and environmental contamination [7]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [8], an organic polymer semiconductor that is highly responsive to visible light, has various advantages, including environmental friendliness, stability, and affordability. These qualities prompt its extensive use in several fields, such as photocatalytic hydrogen production [9,10], CO2 photoreduction [11,12], and degradation of organic pollutants [13,14]. g-C3N4 has a relatively narrow bandgap of approximately 2.79 eV, denoting suitable conduction and valence bands (VBs) [15]. In addition, as a two-dimensional material, g-C3N4 offers ample reactive sites on its surface [8]. However, further applications of g-C3N4 are hindered because of its fast carrier recombination and inadequate use of solar light [16]. As a result, numerous strategies, including coupling with other semiconductors [10], elemental doping [17], fabrication of different morphologies [18], and tuning of crystal structures [19], have been employed to boost the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4.

Sulfate-radical-based advanced oxidation processes have garnered significant interest for wastewater treatment [20]. Under certain conditions, SO4 •− has a higher redox potential and longer half-life than •OH for the complete degradation of most organic pollutants across a broad pH range [21]. SO4 •− is generated by activating peroxymonosulfate (PMS) via ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, thermal and ultrasonic treatment, or transition metals [22]. Among these, the use of transition metals has recently gained prominence, as it does not require external energy input and can achieve high efficiency [23,24]. Fe ions, in particular, are abundant, environmentally friendly, and nontoxic transition metals [25]. However, the leaching of metal ions can lead to secondary pollution in water systems [26]. Heterogeneous catalysts can reduce heavy-metal leaching while preserving the activation efficiency of PMS [27]. Despite improvements with the incorporation of transition-metal ions into g-C3N4 [28], several issues, such as the limited charge-separation efficiency of photocatalysts based on g-C3N4/PMS activators, persist [29].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel carbon nanomaterials that are less than 10 nm in size and are known for their excellent photoluminescent properties, biocompatibility, dispersibility in aqueous solutions, small dimensions, safety, and ease of surface functionalisation [30]. Consequently, they have broad prospects in sensing, energy storage, bioimaging, and photocatalytic applications [31]. Among these, CQD introduction can improve carrier migration in g-C3N4, suppress the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and broaden its response in the visible spectrum [32,33].

Herein, we prepared a composite photocatalyst of g-C3N4 co-decorated with Fe atoms and CQDs (Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4) through a one-step hydrothermal strategy with the application of CBZ degradation under sunlight. Fe was loaded onto the CN aromatic rings through Fe–N bonds, and the co-doping of Fe and CQD with the g-C3N4 interface formed a heterojunction [34,35], thereby promoting the charge-transfer process and enhancing the surface active sites. Compared to the bandgap in g-C3N4 (2.79 eV), the bandgap of the catalyst after doping with Fe and CQDs was substantially narrowed (2.25 eV), further confirming the promotion of photo-induced charge separation. The Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst achieved 100 % CBZ photodegradation in 15 min and possessed commendable chemical stability and reliability for practical applications. Additionally, the pH of zero point of charge (pHzpc) and the influence of the pH value was investigated. Further, the CBZ degradation pathways were elucidated by ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography and high-definition mass spectrometry (UPLC/HDMS).

The Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst was fabricated using a straightforward hydrothermal approach ( Fig. 1). First, g-C3N4 was synthesized using a previously reported technique [8]. Urea was heated in a crucible sealed at 550 °C for 3 h at the heating rate of 2.5 °C/min using a muffle furnace, yielding g-C3N4 upon cooling to room temperature. In 25 mL of ethanol, 12.5 mg each of folic acid (FA) and o-phenylenediamine (oPD), 500 mg of g-C3N4, and varied amounts of anhydrous ferric chloride (1.45, 4.35, and 7.25 mg) were dispersed and labelled as A, B, and C (C-1 %, C-3 %, and C-5 %), respectively. The mixture was heated to 180 °C, maintained at this temperature for 1 h, centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5 min, washed five times alternating between 95 % ethanol and water, and freeze-dried. Finally, the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst was obtained.

The photocatalytic activity of the as-synthesized catalysts was evaluated in a Q-Sun Xe-1 test chamber. The prepared catalyst was added to 20 mL of CBZ solution (2.5 × 10−5 mol/L). The mixture was then dispersed by ultrasonication in the dark for 10 min, and the PMS solution was added to the prepared suspension to initiate the test. Samples were periodically withdrawn from the reaction system every 3 min, filtered with a 0.22-μm Teflon filter membrane to eliminate the solids, and subjected to UPLC with a PDA detector (UPLC, Waters) to determine the CBZ concentration. The solution pH was controlled using H2SO4 and NaOH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH of zero charge of point (pHzpc) of g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4 and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 samples
ce:section.ce:para: The pHzpc of the samples were investigated using the salt addition method. A series of ten NaCl solutions (0.1 mol/L) were prepared with varying pH values, ranging from an initial pH of 2–11. The samples were dispersed in the NaCl solution and shaken for 24 h, after which the final pH value (pHf) was recorded. A graphical representation was constructed to illustrate the relationship between the initial pH value (pHi) and the change in pH (ΔpH).

The morphological and microstructural attributes of Fe–CQD/g-C3N4 were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. S1 presents the SEM micrographs of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4, depicting the retained layered structure of g-C3N4. The TEM images of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 2(a–d). Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 maintains an overlapping layered structure of g-C3N4. Upon doping g-C3N4 with CQDs and Fe atoms, dark spots representing CQDs are observed on the surfaces. The CQDs are uniformly and abundantly distributed on g-C3N4. The average size of the CQDs is 3–4 nm. As Fe atoms were incorporated into the g-C3N4 framework, the Fe atoms could not be detected by TEM, and their presence was confirmed by the subsequent characterisations.

The phases and structures of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4, Fe/CQDs, Fe/g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4, CQDs, and g-C3N4 were analysed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S2, g-C3N4 exhibits a prominent diffraction peak at 27.5°, consistent with the (002) plane and linked to stacked aromatic structures [36]. The diffraction peaks of the CQDs at 21.8° and 26.7° are consistent with the (220) [37] and (002) [38] planes, respectively. Meanwhile, the (002) peak of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 is significantly intensified, indicating the successful introduction of CQDs. No peaks corresponding to Fe are observed in the XRD spectra of any of the catalysts, suggesting that the Fe atoms are highly distributed over g-C3N4, presumably existing as Fe–N bonds. Moreover, modification with Fe and CQDs does not significantly affect the stacked aromatic structures of g-C3N4.

The optical properties of the samples were investigated by diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) ( Fig. 3(a)). The absorption edge of pure g-C3N4 is observed at approximately 440 nm, demonstrating a photonic response within the visible spectrum, consistent with an extensive bandgap energy of 2.79 eV. The absorption edge of the CQDs surpasses that of g-C3N4, signifying a narrower bandgap for the CQDs and notable visible-light absorption. In particular, the binding of the CQDs to g-C3N4 widens the visible-light response, thereby greatly enhancing the visible-light absorption of CQD/g-C3N4. The increased visible-light spectral response of Fe/CQDs and Fe/g-C3N4 compared with those of CQDs and g-C3N4 is induced by the introduction of Fe atoms. After the co-coupling of CQDs and Fe atoms, the visible-light spectral response of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 further increases, indicating the optimised utilisation of visible light and superior photocatalytic performance. Consequently, the incorporation of CQDs and Fe atoms on g-C3N4 promotes light absorption owing to the reduced bandgap and photosensitising effect of the CQDs, along with the absorption of visible light by the Fe atoms, thereby facilitating the generation of photogenerated carriers and demonstrating the potential for photocatalysis under visible-light conditions.

The structure of the catalyst was analysed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy; the results are shown in Fig. 3(b). The spectra of pure g-C3N4 are consistent with those reported in previous studies [39]. In particular, the broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 is ascribed primarily to the terminal –NH stretching vibrations due to the defects in the aromatic rings. The peak at 814 cm−1 corresponds to the tri-s-triazine unit. Moreover, several strong peaks at 1200–1600 cm−1 are associated with the C–N heterocyclic skeletal vibrations of the aromatic (C6N7) ring. The typical graphitic structure of g-C3N4 is preserved after doping with Fe atoms and CQDs. The characteristic peaks of Fe/CQDs are similar to those of the solitary CQDs, indicating that doping with Fe atoms does not affect the chemical architecture of the CQDs. In the FT-IR spectrum, the characteristic peaks of Fe and shift in the peaks of the composite catalysts are not observed because of the low Fe content. Concurrently, the changes in the intensities of the CN heterocycle peaks of the CQDs/g-C3N4 and Fe/g-C3N4 are not significantly discernible. However, the intensity of the CN heterocyclic peaks in Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 decrease, indicating the successful loading of Fe onto the CN aromatic rings via Fe–N bonds.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyse the chemical composition of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4. Fig. S3 displays the presence of C, N, and O in g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4, and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4. The detected O is attributed to the bound water and hydroxyl groups associated with g-C3N4. Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 contains Fe with a less prominent peak because of its low concentration. The C 1 s spectrum of g-C3N4, as shown in Fig. 4(a), exhibits three peaks at 284.80, 286.23, and 288.07 eV, which are associated with C–C, C–N/C–O, and NC–N, respectively [40]. Compared with the peaks attributed to NC–N, the increase in the C–C peaks of CQDs/g-C3N4 because of the abundant C–C bonds of the CQDs denotes successful CQD doping. Meanwhile, the diminished C–C peak of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 is attributed to the binding of Fe to the amide linkages, which disrupts partial C–C bonds.

The N 1 s peak of g-C3N4 splits into a triplet at binding energies of 398.52, 400.00, and 401.05 eV, corresponding to C–NC, N–(C)3, and N–H, respectively (Fig. 4(b)) [40]. For Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4, the C–NC peak shifts to 398.65 eV, suggesting the Fe interaction with the aromatic rings of g-C3N4. The electrons transfer between the delocalised π bonds of g-C3N4, which have a higher electron density, and the Fe d orbitals, reducing the electron density and increasing the binding energy of the XPS peaks, thereby signifying successful Fe incorporation. The O 1 s spectra exhibit two peaks at 532.17 and 533.61 eV (Fig. 4(c)), which are attributed to the chemisorbed oxygen of the surface hydroxyl groups and C–O–C [41], respectively. The introduction of CQDs, which have abundant hydroxy groups, increases the C–OH proportion over CQDs/g-C3N4 and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4. The deconvolution of Fe 2p depicts six peaks (Fig. 4(d)): at 710.64, 714.72, and 717.89 eV as three deconvoluted peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and at 722.93, 726.36, and 730.35 eV as three deconvoluted peaks of Fe 2p1/2 [42]. The XPS findings confirm the successful synthesis of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 with minimal alteration of the chemical structure of g-C3N4, consistent with the FT-IR results.

Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy was used to measure the Fe content in 3 % Fe–CQDs, 3 % Fe/g-C3N4, and 3 % Fe–CQD/g-C3N4 (Table S1). In 3 % Fe/CQDs and 3 % Fe/g-C3N4, the Fe content is approximately 1 %, suggesting the successful Fe doping of CQDs and g-C3N4. In the 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 sample, the Fe content is approximately 0.3 %, which is lower than the theoretical value. However, the photocatalytic activity of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 is markedly higher than that of Fe/CQDs and Fe/g-C3N4. These results suggest the intensified collaborative interplay among Fe, CQDs, and g-C3N4 upon their incorporation, thereby markedly improving the photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic activities of the synthesized catalysts under solar irradiation were assessed using CBZ as the model pollutant. Fig. 5(a) shows CBZ photodegradation using different catalysts, indicating the optimal photocatalytic activity of 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4. Moreover, the effects of the Fe content of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 on CBZ photodegradation were investigated (Fig. 5(b)). The photocatalytic activities of catalysts with 3 % and 5 % Fe outperform those with 1 % Fe. The minimal difference in the photocatalytic activity and energy efficiency of 5 % and 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 determines 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 to be the optimal catalyst. To better compare the performance of the prepared catalysts, the degradation process of CBZ was fitted with the pseudo-first-order model ln(C/C0) = -kt. As shown in Fig. S4(a), the reaction rate constant of 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 was 0.403 min−1, which was significantly greater than those of g-C3N4 and other composite catalysts. The reaction rate constants for 1 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 and 5 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 were 0.160 min−1 and 0.320 min−1, respectively, indicating that 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 was the optimal catalyst. Therefore, only 3 % Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 (hereafter referred to as Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4) was analysed in the subsequent methods. Fig. 5(c) shows the effect of varying dosages of the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 photocatalyst on the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ. The CBZ degradation rate progressively increases as the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 dosage is increased from 0.05 g/L to 0.20 g/L. A minimal increase in photocatalytic activity is noted as the catalyst dosage is increased from 0.10 g/L to 0.20 g/L; thus, the optimal dosage is determined to be 0.10 g/L. The effects of PMS and light on CBZ photodegradation by the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts are shown in Fig. 5(d). In 15 min, the CBZ photodegradation efficiency using the catalyst with no additions (designated “none” in the figure) and using Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 without light irradiation are 0.9 % and 1.6 %, respectively. Meanwhile, the CBZ photodegradation efficiency using Fe–CQD/g-C3N4 without PMS is 15.3 %, whereas that with PMS reaches 100 %, indicating that Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 effectively utilises PMS to rapidly degrade CBZ. The total organic carbon (TOC) photodegradation efficiency of CBZ over Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 is approximately 21.24 % under solar irradiation for 15 min. This result further confirms the CBZ mineralisation ability of the photocatalytic system.

Fig. 6(a) shows the effect of PMS concentration on the CBZ photodegradation efficiency using Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4. With PMS concentrations of 0.3 and 0.4 mM, CBZ is completely removed by Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4. For cost effectiveness, the optimal PMS concentration was selected as 0.3 mM. The influence of various anions on CBZ degradation through photocatalysis using Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS was also explored. Fig. 6(b) illustrates the CBZ photodegradation efficiency with different anions, which follow the sequence: NaCl > NaNO3 > Na2SO4 > Na2CO3. The influence of the Cl− concentration on the photocatalytic performance is detailed in Fig. S5. The photocatalytic activity is gradually enhanced with increasing Cl− concentration.

The role of pH on the photocatalytic activity for CBZ degradation by the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS system under varying pH values was examined. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the CBZ photodegradation efficiency of 100 % is obtained at pH 3 and 5, whereas the CBZ photodegradation efficiencies are 96.2 % and 93 % at pH 7 and 9, respectively, demonstrating the excellent photocatalytic performance of the system across a broad pH range. Further, the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 system has an elevated ability to degrade CBZ under acidic conditions.

The pHzpc of g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4 and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 are depicted in Fig. 6(d). The pHzpc of g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4 and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 are 5.1, 5.3 and 5.7, respectively; the surface charge of the catalyst is positive when the pH is below the pHzpc, and negative when the pH exceeds that value. CBZ, possessing two pKa values, is capable of dissociating at two distinct functional groups depending on the pH of the solution. These pKa values are 13.9 (for the amine group) and 2.3 (for the ketone group) [43]. Consequently, under more acidic conditions, the ketone group of carbamazepine may carry a positive charge, whereas the amine group may bear a negative charge under alkaline conditions. Therefore, in the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS degradation system for CBZ, adsorption does not play a dominant role. Given that PMS exhibits superior performance under acidic conditions, the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS system demonstrates a higher capacity for the degradation of CBZ under acidic conditions, which is consistent with the results shown in Fig. 6(c).

The photocatalytic performance of the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst was tested with the degradation of different substrates, namely sulfamethazine (SMZ), sulfachloropyridazine (SCP), sulfadiazine (SD), sulfaquinoxaline sodium (SQXNa), and rhodamine B (RhB), under simulated sunlight ( Fig. 7(a)). The degradation rates are 99.2 % for SMZ in 25 min, 100 % for SCP in 10 min, 99.8 % for SD in 20 min, 99 % for SQXNa in 10 min, and 99.4 % for RhB in 40 min. Therefore, the Fe–CQD/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst has effective photocatalytic degradation over a wide range of substrates, demonstrating its outstanding photocatalytic performance.

As shown in Fig. 7(b), the deactivation of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 is not discernible after six consecutive 15-min cycles of CBZ degradation under solar irradiation, indicating the good chemical stability and reliability of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 for practical applications. The leached Fe ions were investigated, as shown in Fig. S6. With 0.3 mM PMS, the leachate had a CBZ photodegradation efficiency of 17 %, whereas the self-degradation of CBZ and photocatalytic activity of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 resulted in photodegradation efficiency of 10 % and 100 %, respectively. These results suggest that Fe leaching is minimal in the photocatalytic system, confirming the high stability of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4.

The used Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 catalysts were recovered after degradation experiments and characterized using FT-IR (Fig. S7(a)) and XPS (Fig. S7(b-f)), with comparisons made to the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst before degradation experiments. Fig. S7(a) shows that the used photocatalytic material retained its distribution of functional groups effectively, indicating the excellent stability of the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst. The C 1 s and N 1 s spectra of the used Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibit minimal change (Fig. S7(c) and (d)), suggesting that the triazine units within g-C3N4 and the interactive chemical bonds within the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 system were well-preserved. The O 1 s spectrum depicted in Fig. S7(e) shows a slight shift of the C–OH peak to a higher binding energy and a decrease in peak intensity, indicating that various oxygen-containing radicals played a crucial role in the photodegradation of CBZ. In the Fe 2p spectrum of the used Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4, similar characteristic peaks can be discerned (Fig. S7(f)). However, the intensity of these peaks was reduced compared to those of the fresh Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4, which may be associated with the loss of doped iron during the reaction process. These results further confirm the structural stability of the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 catalyst.

The photogenerated charge recombination behaviour of the g-C3N4, Fe/g-C3N4, and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 samples at an emission wavelength of approximately 440 nm was analysed using the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum ( Fig. 8(a)). Compared to the spectrum of g-C3N4, the emission peak intensity of Fe/g-C3N4 is diminished, indicating that the incorporation of Fe atoms restrains the charge recombination rate of g-C3N4. The introduction of CQDs further attenuates the emission peak intensity of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 owing to the distinct chemical and optical attributes of CQDs, suggesting that the CQDs could act as electron acceptors. These findings indicate that doping with Fe atoms and CQDs effectively suppresses charge recombination and enhances charge transfer, with the largest advantage obtained from the synergistic effect between the two phenomena.

Transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy are effective methods for demonstrating the transfer efficiencies of photogenerated charges. As shown in Fig. 8(b), Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 exhibits a higher photocurrent intensity than the other catalysts. This increased intensity indicates the enhanced photocurrent response with the introduction of Fe and CQDs, which effectively promotes the separation and transfer efficiencies of the photogenerated charges. As shown in Fig. 8(c)), Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 has a lower resistance and faster charge-transfer capabilities than g-C3N4 and Fe/g-C3N4 have. The improvement of these properties suggests the enhanced interfacial charge transfer of the catalyst with the introduction of Fe and CQDs, facilitating the generation of photoinduced charges and active species.

The bandgaps of the samples were calculated using DRS; the results are presented in Fig. 8(d). The calculated bandgap widths of g-C3N4 and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 are 2.79 and 2.25 eV, respectively. Doping with Fe and CQDs narrows the bandgap of the catalyst (2.25 eV) compared to that of g-C3N4, promoting the separation of the photoinduced charge. In addition, the VB positions of the catalysts were investigated using XPS (Fig. S8), revealing the VB of g-C3N4 at 1.78 eV, which provides robust support for investigation into the photocatalytic mechanism of the catalyst.

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spin-trapping techniques were employed during the photocatalytic reactions to study the potential reactive free radicals involved. 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used as the trapping agent during CBZ degradation to capture 1O2 [44]. The 1O2 signal is detected upon irradiating aqueous dispersions of g-C3N4, CQDs/g-C3N4, and Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 for 2 min, confirming the presence of 1O2 as an active species ( Fig. 9(a)). •O2 −, SO4 •−, and •OH could be captured using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent [45]. Four distinguishing peaks are indexed to DMPO-•O2 − under solar irradiation, as shown in Fig. 9(b). Compared with its production in the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 system, the •O2 − production during the photocatalytic reactions of g-C3N4 and CQDs/g-C3N4 is lower, which is likely attributed to the charge transfer involving g-C3N4, CQDs, and Fe. Quintessential signals of •OH and SO4 •− are also detected, as shown in Fig. 9(c), with spectral intensities ranking in the order of Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 < CQDs/g-C3N4 < g-C3N4. For all catalysts examined, the intensity of •OH surpasses that of SO4 •−, because the •OH species can be produced from SO4 •− reacting with H2O or •OH [46]. Overall, the EPR analyses obtained 1O2, •O2 −, •OH, and SO4 •− as the active species in the photocatalytic Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS reaction system.

The hole (h+) is typically considered the advantageous active species of g-C3N4 [8]. Hence, potassium iodide (KI) has been used to capture h+ [8], p-benzoquinone (BQ) to capture •O2 − and 1O2, L-histidine to capture 1O2, tertiary-butanol (TBA) to capture •OH [47], and ethanol (EtOH) to capture SO4 •− [44]. As shown in Fig. 9(d), the CBZ photodegradation efficiency from the control solution is 100 % after 15 min. The addition of TBA reduces the CBZ photodegradation efficiency to 81.9 %, indicating a lower •OH yield. Similarly, the CBZ photodegradation efficiency decreases to 40.6 %, 10.7 %, 7.3 %, and 1.6 % with the addition of EtOH, L-histidine, BQ, and KI, respectively, highlighting the significant roles of SO4 •−, 1O2, •O2 −, and h+ in CBZ degradation; these observations correspond with the EPR results.

Based on the aforementioned results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism of the degradation of target substrates in the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS system was proposed. As shown in Fig. 10, under solar light, g-C3N4 is excited, producing electrons and holes. The incorporation of Fe atoms and CQDs expedites the conduction velocity of the electrons and holes, thereby enhancing the solar-light utilisation and generating additional photoinduced carriers. Owing to the electron-accepting function of CQDs and charge-capture sites of N defects, the photogenerated carriers are effectively migrated to the photocatalyst surface, thereby improving the charge-separation efficiency. The surface electrons combine with O to produce •O2 −. Meanwhile, PMS is effectively activated to produce SO4 •− and •OH [48], and SO5 •− further generates 1O2 [45]. Therefore, the photocatalytic process involving h+, •O2 −, 1O2, •OH, and SO4 •− realised the excellent photocatalytic performance of the Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4/PMS system.

UPLC/HDMS was used to investigate the possible intermediates of CBZ degradation in the system ( Fig. 11). In the positive mode, the retention time of CBZ is 4.80 min with a peak at m/z = 237.1028. Ten intermediates were detected, as listed in Table S2. For the CBZ molecule, the olefinic double bond is the primary reaction site [49]. In the mass spectrum at m/z = 253.0977, the degradation product 10,11-epoxycarbamazepine (C1) is generated by the attack of the reactive O species on the olefinic double bond in the central heterocycle of CBZ, which can serve as a precursor to other intermediates [50]. Subsequently, C1 undergoes further oxidation via four distinct pathways. In pathway I, C1 is transformed into C2 (m/z = 208.0762) via facile ring contraction and amide group loss [51]. C2 is transformed into C6 through aldehyde group loss, and C6 is further oxidised by singlet oxygen to yield C9 [52]. For pathways II and III, C1 is further oxidised to produce C3 and C4, respectively. Consequently, C3 (m/z = 251.0821) transforms into C7 via amide loss. In pathway IV, C1 is hydrolysed to produce C5 (m/z = 271.1083), which then undergoes heterocycle ring opening to form a dialdehyde compound [53]. The unstable dialdehyde is further hydrolysed to C8. The reactive species generated by the photocatalytic activity reacted with C8 to form the carboxylic product C10. The intermediate products are eventually mineralised into CO2 and H2O through further oxidation and ring-opening reactions by reactive species [50,54], which is consistent with the TOC results.

The Fe–CQDs/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst was synthesized by co-decorating g-C3N4 with Fe atoms and CQDs. Under solar irradiation, the Fe–CQD/g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst activated PMS proficiently, and the degradation efficiency of CBZ reached nearly 100 %. The introduction of Fe and CQD reduces the band gap of the catalyst from the original 2.79 eV of g-C3N4 to 2.25 eV, which is beneficial for the separation of photogenerated charges. Additionally, the Fe–CQD/g-C3N4/PMS system exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity over a broad pH range (3–9) and superior environmental adaptability. The reactive species for CBZ degradation were determined to be h+, •O2 −, 1O2, •OH, and SO4 •−. Furthermore, the degradation pathway of CBZ was proposed based on the UPLC/HDMS results. In summary, this study highlights an original approach for designing efficient composite photocatalysts for the rational utilisation of PMS and degradation of pollutants in a short duration and at a low cost for environmental remediation.
